literal: dependent co-arising; from prati-i 'to depend on, to come back to' + sam-ut-√pad 'to arise together'
invariant_definition: *Pratītya-samutpāda* names the principle that nothing arises in isolation — every existent is the joint outcome of conditions other than itself. In the early Buddhist *abhidharma* the principle is given concrete form in the twelve-membered chain (*dvādaśa-aṅga*: *avidyā* → *saṃskāra* → *vijñāna* → *nāma-rūpa* → *ṣaḍ-āyatana* → *sparśa* → *vedanā* → *tṛṣṇā* → *upādāna* → *bhava* → *jāti* → *jarā-maraṇa*). In Nāgārjuna's Mādhyamaka the principle is identified with *śūnyatā* itself: at MMK 24.18, *yaḥ pratītya-samutpādaḥ śūnyatāṃ tāṃ pracakṣmahe* — "that which is dependent co-arising, that we declare to be emptiness." The identification fixes the Mādhyamaka reading of emptiness: to lack *svabhāva* is precisely to arise in dependence on conditions, and the doctrine of dependent arising is not a doctrine of how *svabhāva* bearing entities causally produce one another but a doctrine that there are no *svabhāva* bearing entities to do the producing.

per_school.definition: Advaita engages *pratītya-samutpāda* at *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.2.18-25 as the Buddhist thesis that *citta-caitta*-s arise by depending on causal conditions within a *kṣaṇika-santāna*, and Śaṅkara argues that such a model either abandons momentariness or destroys determinate causal order. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.22-vainasika-nirodha] The decisive objection is *pratyabhijñā* and *anusmṛti*: the judgment `aham ado 'drākṣam idaṃ paśyāmi` requires an enduring subject, so a merely dependently serial succession cannot account for experience, memory, or mokṣa. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.2.25-anusmrti]

Madhva reconstructs the Buddhist position as a momentary world and an aggregate of atoms conditioned by time and karma, with the five-skandha continuum treated as the locus of bondage and release. [cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.172-175-bauddha-paksa] Tattva-vāda rejects this by appeal to *pratyabhijñā* and the inferable stability of entities, and it further treats *śūnyavāda* as a nirviśeṣa conclusion that cannot sustain real difference. [cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.184-pratyabhijna] [cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.2.230-sunyavada]

Sāṃkhya construes origination through *satkāryavāda*: the effect is real as precontained in its cause, so production is manifestation or *pariṇāma* rather than the rise of an intrinsically new, condition-only event. [cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/9] The *Sāṃkhya-Sūtra* accordingly rejects *kṣaṇikatva*, *vijñānamātra*, and *śūnyatā* by appeal to *pratyabhijñā*, the impossibility of a stable effect under sheer momentariness, and the givenness of external objects. [cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.34-1.44-kṣaṇikatva-vijñānamātra-śūnyatā]

Where Yoga confronts the Buddhist thesis of momentary dependent cognition, Vyāsa argues that a *citta* made of `pratyayamātra` and `kṣaṇika` episodes would make *ekāgratā*, *vikṣepa*, memory, and *karmāśaya* continuity unintelligible. [cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.32-kṣaṇika-citta-nirāsa] Yoga therefore explains continuity through one enduring *citta*'s *pariṇāma*-s rather than through a merely serial production of mutually vanishing moments. [cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.32-kṣaṇika-citta-nirāsa]

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika addresses the Buddhist background of *pratītya-samutpāda* by opening a *kṣaṇabhaṅga* examination and by later rejecting *sarvaśūnyatā* through the insistence that *bhāva*-s are established by *svabhāva*. [cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.10-17-kṣaṇabhaṅga] [cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.37-40] Vātsyāyana's bhāṣya makes the same point against momentariness: if entities were utterly momentary, causal regularity and realist ontology could not be maintained. [cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/3.2.10-kṣaṇikatva]

For Mādhyamaka, *pratītya-samutpāda* is precisely *śūnyatā*, since whatever arises dependently is devoid of *svabhāva* and is therefore only *upādāya-prajñapti* within the *madhyamā pratipad*. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18] [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19] [cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/22] Nāgārjuna accordingly treats seeing dependent arising as seeing duḥkha, samudaya, nirodha, and mārga themselves, not as discovering an occult causal substrate beneath phenomena. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.40]

Yogācāra internalizes dependent arising as *paratantra-svabhāva*: the flow of *vikalpa* is *pratyayodbhava*, arising from conditions rather than from self-grounded external substances. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/21] The perfected nature is just the perpetual absence within that dependent stream of the imagined duality, so *pratītya-samutpāda* is read through the *trisvabhāva* analysis rather than through external causal realism. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/20] [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/22]

per_school.not_applicable_reason: No on-disk grammatical primaries were supplied for this phase, and the school is not being indexed here as having a distinct substantive doctrine under the specifically Buddhist heading `pratītya-samutpāda`.

translator_note: The standard glosses 'dependent origination' and 'dependent arising' are both acceptable; 'co-arising' captures the *sam* prefix. The principle is widely misread in popular sources as a Buddhist doctrine of universal causation; in its Mādhyamaka use the *positive* causal claim is precisely what the doctrine refuses, since causation in any *svabhāva* realist sense would presuppose entities whose *svabhāva* the doctrine denies.
